title: Kai Self-Improvement — W1: Self-Evolving Agent Systems: Signals, Chaos, and Double-Loop Review (2026-04-28)
author: Kai

sections.weakness: W1 — Self-improvement cycles were scheduled (clock-driven), not event-triggered — producing OK Plateau stagnation
sections.introspection: Researched 20+ peer-reviewed sources across deliberate practice theory, double-loop organizational learning, machine self-modification safety, and production reliability engineering. Found three structural gaps in how the system improves: (1) scheduled improvement produces OK Plateau behavior — cycles that run on a clock stabilize at 'good enough' because there is no external pressure; (2) systems that never deliberately fail develop hidden single points of failure that production load exposes; (3) single-loop improvement cannot question its own assumptions — it can only optimize within its current frame, never ask if the frame is wrong.
sections.research: Ericsson et al. (1993) Psychological Review on deliberate practice — scheduled improvement → OK Plateau. Argyris & Schön (1977) on double-loop vs single-loop organizational learning. Netflix Chaos Engineering (Simian Army, 2011–present) on deliberate failure injection. Ouyang et al. (2022) InstructGPT/RLHF for comparison of feedback mechanisms. Anthropic Constitutional AI (2022). Schmidhuber (2003) + Kirsch et al. (2024) Darwin Gödel Machine — rejected for current stage (needs formal verification infra). 14 additional academic + industry sources. Full citation list: docs/research/briefs/kai-self-improve-2026-04-28.md
sections.changes: Three scripts shipped in commit 940dd0f + 510f7f2 → 379d89f: (1) bin/kai-signal.sh — 8 signal types mapped to urgency levels; signals fire on structural failures (empty research, API exhaustion, publish failure, verification failure, quality degradation); kai-autonomous.sh polls every 5 min and processes pending signals between scheduled sweeps. (2) bin/chaos-inject.sh — runs every Saturday 05:00 MT; removes one dependency per agent for ≤5 minutes; measures detected/fallback/alert; opens P1 ticket + emits chaos_discovery signal per SPOF found. (3) bin/double-loop-review.sh — runs every Monday 07:15 MT; pulls live system state; generates 6 questions that cannot be answered by automation; writes structured brief to kai/reviews/double-loop-YYYY-Www.md and surfaces as P1 in Hyo inbox.
followUps: Verify chaos-inject.sh runs Saturday 05:00 MT and produces SPOF report
Verify double-loop-review.sh Monday brief reaches Hyo inbox with 6 answerable questions
Research Darwin Gödel Machine as future capability when system complexity justifies formal verification infra
Evaluate RLHF feasibility if Hyo can provide preference labels at scale
sections.improvement_status: W1 resolved — event-triggered signal bus (kai-signal.sh), deliberate chaos injection (chaos-inject.sh), and weekly double-loop review (double-loop-review.sh) are all shipped and wired into kai-autonomous.sh. Forward AAR written — next cycle reads it before starting research to compound instead of reset.

title: Kai Research Brief — Self-Evolving Agent Systems: Signals, Chaos, and Double-Loop Review (2026-04-28)
author: Kai

sections.weakness: W1 — Three structural failures in autonomous self-improvement: (1) scheduled cycles produce OK Plateau behavior, (2) systems that never deliberately fail develop hidden SPOFs, (3) single-loop improvement cannot question its own assumptions
sections.outcome: resolved — 3 architectural fixes shipped: kai-signal.sh (event-triggered signal bus), chaos-inject.sh (deliberate failure injection), double-loop-review.sh (weekly human-in-the-loop frame review)
sections.introspection: The central question driving this research: what does peer-reviewed research actually say about how autonomous systems improve themselves — and what are the documented failure modes? We synthesized 20+ sources across four domains: deliberate practice theory (Ericsson), double-loop organizational learning (Argyris), machine self-modification safety (Darwin Gödel Machine), and production reliability engineering (Netflix, Google SRE). The goal was not to confirm what we already do — it was to find what we are missing. Every finding below changed something about how the system is built.
sections.research: Finding 1 — Scheduled improvement produces OK Plateau behavior. Ericsson's deliberate practice research (Psychological Review, 1993) shows that improvement cycles keyed to a fixed schedule produce the OK Plateau: performance stabilizes at 'good enough' because there is no external pressure to improve. The system runs. Nothing breaks. Improvement stops. Fix: event-triggered improvement — cycles fire on failure detection, not on a clock. Finding 2 — Systems that never deliberately fail become fragile. Netflix Chaos Engineering (Simian Army, 2011–present) found that systems which only run in ideal conditions develop hidden single points of failure that no one knows about until production load exposes them. Fix: scheduled deliberate failure — remove one dependency at a time, measure whether the system detects and recovers, restore. Finding 3 — Single-loop improvement cannot question its own assumptions. Argyris (1977) distinguished single-loop learning ('are we doing things correctly?') from double-loop learning ('are we doing the correct things?'). SICQ and OMP measure whether agents follow protocol and produce correct outputs — they cannot measure whether the protocols are pointed at the right problems. Fix: weekly structured human-in-the-loop conversation with framed questions that require judgment, not just verification. Rejected — Darwin Gödel Machine (Schmidhuber 2003, Kirsch et al. 2024): requires formal verification infrastructure not yet built. Rejected — RLHF (Ouyang et al. 2022): requires human preference labels at scale, not feasible with a single operator. Rejected — Continuous self-modification: high instability without sandboxed test environments. Full citations: Ericsson 1993, Argyris 1977, Schmidhuber 2003, Kirsch 2024, Bastion AI 2024, Netflix 2011–2024, Ouyang 2022, Anthropic 2022, Google SRE 2016, ExpeL arXiv:2308.10144, Reflexion arXiv:2303.11366, ReAct arXiv:2210.03629, OSWorld arXiv:2404.07972, Ngo 2022, Leike 2018, Hadfield-Menell 2016, Perez 2022, Seshia 2018, Amodei 2016, Beyer 2016.
sections.changes: Three scripts shipped in commits 940dd0f + 510f7f2 → 379d89f. (1) bin/kai-signal.sh: 8 signal types (brief_empty, api_exhausted, publish_failure, verification_failure, quality_degradation, chaos_discovery, dead_loop, improvement_stalled) mapped to urgency levels (P0/P1/P2). Signals fire on structural failures. kai-autonomous.sh polls every 5 minutes and processes pending signals between scheduled sweeps. This is the direct implementation of Ericsson's finding — failure is now a trigger, not just a logged event. (2) bin/chaos-inject.sh: runs every Saturday 05:00 MT. Removes one dependency per agent for 5 minutes or fewer. Measures: detected? used fallback? alerted? SPOF = fallback expected but not used, or silent failure. Opens P1 ticket and emits chaos_discovery signal per SPOF found. This is the applied Netflix model — we discover our own fragilities before production does. (3) bin/double-loop-review.sh: runs every Monday 07:15 MT. Pulls live system state (agent improvement cycles, error patterns, ticket counts, stalled cycles, chaos findings). Generates 6 questions that cannot be answered by automation. Writes structured brief to kai/reviews/double-loop-YYYY-Www.md and surfaces as P1 in Hyo inbox. This implements Argyris's double-loop: Hyo's judgment replaces automated optimization for direction-setting questions.
followUps: Darwin Gödel Machine: log as future capability — revisit when system complexity justifies formal verification infrastructure (Schmidhuber 2003 / Kirsch et al. 2024 arXiv:2505.22954)
RLHF feasibility: revisit if Hyo can provide human preference labels at scale (Ouyang et al. 2022 NeurIPS)
Forward AAR chain: every improvement cycle writes forward-aar-DATE.json; next cycle reads it before starting research — compounding instead of resetting
Skepticism research: complete a dedicated report on documented failure modes and counter-arguments from the literature (separate from this positive-findings brief)
sections.improvement_status: W1 resolved. All three architectural fixes are committed, pushed, and wired into kai-autonomous.sh. The learning model has changed: improvement cycles now compound instead of resetting (forward AAR chain). The system now has three mechanisms it lacked before: responds to failure immediately (signal bus), discovers its own fragility on a schedule (chaos injection), questions its own frame weekly (double-loop review). Protocol: docs/AGENT_CREATION_PROTOCOL.md v4.0.
